project.title: LC-MS of LAMP5 in human multiple myeloma cells
project.projectDescription: Multiple myeloma (MM)is the second most common tumor of the hematologic system. MM remains incurable at this time. In this study, we used bioinformatics analysis to find key genes in the pathogenesis of MM. We first found that Lysosome associated membrane protein5 (LAMP5) expression was sequentially increased in Multiple myeloma.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Our study employs a meticulous sample processing protocol to ensure the integrity and representativeness of the analyzed samples. Initial steps involve homogenization of the biological samples followed by protein extraction using a lysis buffer. We incorporate a series of centrifugation steps to separate and enrich the protein fraction of interest. Finally, the samples are prepared for LC-MS/MS analysis, with quality control checks at each step to ensure sample purity and concentration.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: samples were subjected to in-solution trypsin digestion and dried. Samples were analyzed on a EASY-nLC 1200（Thermo SCIENTIFIC) coupled to a Orbitrap Fusion Lumos （Thermo SCIENTIFIC)equipped with an EASY-IC ion source. Peptides were dissolved in 10 ul 0.1% formic acid and were auto-sampled directly onto a homemade C18 column (35cm X 75μm i.d., 2.5μm 100Å). Samples were then eluted for 120mins with linear gradients of 3–35% acetonitrile in 0.1% formic acid at a flow rate of 300 nl/min.  The raw files were analyzed by Proteome Discoverer 2.5 software against uniprot database.

references.referenceLine: Li Z, Liu R, Fang Z, Chen R, Yang D, Li Y, Liu S, Wang C, Liu H. LAMP5 modulates IRF4 stability and nuclear transport: a critical mechanism in myeloma progression and therapy. Oncogene. 2025 44(38):3553-3567
